title: Variables & Types
description: Master C# value types, reference types, casting, nullable types, and const/readonly for interview coding.

sections.title: Primitive Types: int, long, double, string, bool
sections.explanation: C# has built-in value types: `int` (32-bit), `long` (64-bit, suffix L), `double` (64-bit float), `bool` (true/false), and the reference type `string`. Declare with the type keyword or `var` for implicit typing. Value types live on the stack and have default values (0, 0.0, false). `string` defaults to null. In interviews, stick to explicit types for clarity.

exercise.prompt: Declare an int named `age` set to 25, a long named `population` set to 8000000000L, a double named `rate` set to 0.075, a bool named `valid` set to false, and a string named `label` set to "test". Print each on its own line.

validation.message: Print each variable on its own line with the exact values specified.
Use the correct type for each variable: int, long, double, bool, string.

exercise.hint: Declare each variable with its type keyword. Remember the L suffix on long literals. C# prints bool as True/False.

sections.title: Type Casting
sections.explanation: C# does implicit widening (int → long → double) automatically. Narrowing (double → int) requires an explicit cast with `(type)`. Integer division truncates: `7 / 2` gives `3`. To get a double result, cast at least one operand: `(double)7 / 2` gives `3.5`. Use `Convert.ToInt32()` for rounding or `int.Parse()` / `int.TryParse()` for string-to-int conversion.

exercise.prompt: Given int variables `num = 10` and `den = 3`, print the integer division result, then the double division result (cast num to double). Then parse the string "42" to an int and print it plus 8.

validation.message: First line: integer division (3). Second line: double division. Third line: 42 + 8 = 50.
Use explicit cast (double) for widening and int.Parse() for string conversion.

exercise.hint: Use (double)num / den for floating division. Use int.Parse("42") for string-to-int.

sections.title: Null and Nullable Types
sections.explanation: Value types (int, bool, etc.) cannot be null by default. Add `?` to make them nullable: `int? x = null`. Use `.HasValue` and `.Value` to check and extract. The null-coalescing operator `??` provides a default: `x ?? 0`. For reference types like string, null is the default. In interviews, always null-check before accessing members to avoid `NullReferenceException`.

exercise.prompt: Create a nullable int `score` set to null. Print whether it has a value. Then set it to 95 and print the value. Create another nullable int `bonus` left as null and use ?? to default it to 0, printing the result.

validation.message: Use int? for nullable, .HasValue, .Value, and ?? operator.

exercise.hint: int? score = null; then score.HasValue is False. Assign score = 95; then score.Value is 95. int? bonus = null; bonus ?? 0 gives 0.

sections.title: Const and Readonly
sections.explanation: `const` declares compile-time constants—must be assigned at declaration, implicitly static, and only for primitive types and strings. `readonly` declares runtime constants—can be assigned in the constructor, work with any type. Use `const` for true constants (Math values, config limits). Use `readonly` when the value is determined at runtime. In interviews, prefer `const` for simple literals.

exercise.prompt: Declare a const int `MaxSize` set to 100 and a const double `Pi` set to 3.14159. Declare a static readonly string `AppName` set to "CodePilot". In Main, print all three on separate lines.

validation.message: Print MaxSize (100), Pi (3.14159), and AppName on separate lines.
Use const for compile-time constants and static readonly for the runtime constant.

exercise.hint: const int MaxSize = 100; const double Pi = 3.14159; static readonly string AppName = "CodePilot";

sections.title: Type Inference and Checking
sections.explanation: Use `var` for implicit typing when the type is obvious from the right side. Use `is` for type checking and `GetType()` to inspect types at runtime. Pattern matching with `is` can declare a variable inline: `if (obj is string s)`. In interviews, explicit types are often clearer, but `var` is fine for long generic types like `Dictionary<string, List<int>>`.

exercise.prompt: Use `var` to declare an int set to 10, a string set to "hello", and a List<int>. Print the type name of each using .GetType().Name. Then create an object holding the integer 99 and use pattern matching (`is int n`) to extract and print it.

validation.message: Type names: Int32, String, List`1. Then the extracted value 99.
Use var for declarations, GetType().Name for type inspection, and is for pattern matching.

exercise.hint: var x = 10; Console.WriteLine(x.GetType().Name); prints Int32. object obj = 99; if (obj is int n) prints n.
